title: There's a Fourth Color of Karma Nobody Told You About
hook: You were taught karma comes in 'good' and 'bad.' Patanjali quietly added a third option — and it's the only one that sets you free.

script.visual: Two jars on a table labeled GOOD and BAD, filling and spilling marbles into each other. A third, empty, clear jar sits behind them. Person's hand reaches past the first two toward the empty one.
script.voiceover: You were taught two kinds of karma. Patanjali is about to show you a third.

script.visual: Sanskrit: कर्माशुक्लाकृष्णं योगिनस्त्रिविधमितरेषाम्. Person traces the word 'ashukla-akrishnam.'
script.voiceover: Sutra 4.7. He says most people's karma is threefold — white, black, and mixed. Good deeds. Bad deeds. And the usual messy blend. But the yogi's karma? Ashukla-akrishnam. Neither white nor black. Colorless.

script.visual: Person doing two identical actions — writing a thoughtful message — in two different lighting scenarios. One version: they keep checking the screen for likes, smiling anxiously. Second: they send and close the phone, walk away.
script.voiceover: Here is the surgical point. It is not the action that colors your karma. It is the motive. You donate to look generous — that is white karma, and you still owe the ledger. You donate for revenge on your ex who called you stingy — that is black, sort of, with extra steps. You donate because the thing in front of you needed help and you did not think about yourself at all — colorless. No residue. No debt.
script.text_overlay: Same action. Different motive. Different color.

script.visual: Close-up on a ledger being written in. White ink. Black ink. Then a clear pen that writes — and the paper stays blank.
script.voiceover: Your karma ledger records intention, not behavior. White karma gets you to heaven eventually. Black karma gets you the opposite. Both still chain you to the ledger. The yogi's move is not to become extremely white. It is to stop writing in the book at all. You act. The action lands. There is no little voice underneath going 'this will look good' or 'they owed me.' That silence is freedom.
script.text_overlay: The yogi stops writing in the book.

script.visual: Person at their desk, does a small kind thing for a colleague. Does not post it. Does not tell anyone. Keeps working.
script.voiceover: Most of your so-called good deeds are still writing white ink. Patanjali is pointing to the clear pen.
script.text_overlay: Most 'good' still writes in ink. Find the clear pen.

script.voiceover: Name one good thing you did today that was secretly for applause. No judgment. Just see it.
script.text_overlay: One 'good deed' that was secretly for applause?

cta: Name one 'good' thing you did today that had a secret motive. Not to shame yourself — just to see the ink color. Comment below.
caption:
You were taught karma comes in two colors. White (good) and black (bad). Do good things, get good outcomes. Simple.

Patanjali is about to complicate this in one sutra.

Sutra 4.7: कर्माशुक्लाकृष्णं योगिनस्त्रिविधमितरेषाम्
karma-ashukla-akrishnam yoginas trividham itaresham

"The yogi's karma is neither white nor black. For others, it is threefold."

There is a third option. Ashukla-akrishnam. Colorless. Transparent. No residue.

Here is the move: it is not the action that gets color — it is the motive. You donate to be seen as generous — that is white karma, and you still owe the ledger a payout. You donate to spite someone — mostly black. You donate because the person in front of you needed help and the little voice checking how you looked was not running — colorless. Nothing to collect on. Nothing to repay.

White karma chains you to the ledger, just in a nicer handwriting. Black karma chains you to the ledger in worse handwriting. Both still chain you.

The yogi's goal is not to become maximally white. It is to stop writing in the book at all.

This reframes 'good deeds.' Most of what we call good is just a more socially rewarding flavor of bondage. Patanjali is pointing to the pen with no ink — the action done without a secret motive asking for a receipt.

#yogasutras #patanjali #karma #yogaphilosophy #intentions #mindfulness #consciousness #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #ego #authenticity #spirituality #selfawareness #motivation #philosophy
music_suggestion: Neutral, clean piano for the opening. Layers a second piano line at the motive reveal. Strips back to a single silent beat for 'colorless' — the music gets out of the way.
